text: 145 BPM speed garage. Suggested tempo lane is roughly 140-150 BPM.
Start from a brass / orchestral stab and pitch it down to the root note. Pitching it down also slows the sample's internal movement so it fits the beat better.

Main stab processing: OTT to bring out mids and highs, delay, and aggressive high-end EQ used as sound-design EQ rather than normal corrective EQ. Sidechain it to the kick with a filtered detector so the ducking focuses on the top end of the kick and feels tighter.

Vocal atmosphere: stretch a vocal, pitch it into key, reverb it heavily so it becomes atmosphere, tighten the reverb size if needed, high-pass it while keeping the useful fundamental area, then filtered-sidechain it.
Define the chord progression first — F minor, C minor, C# major, D# major — then build the bassline from the root notes of those chords.
Use octave jumps to create swing and movement, and keep the bass notes slightly separated instead of fully touching so the line feels tighter and groovier.

Bass patch in Operator: two sine waves FM-ing each other, one an octave up and slightly detuned, run through a low-pass filter with key tracking turned off so the cutoff stays fixed across the keyboard.
Create bass width with a separate return layer: saturate it, high-pass the lows, add chorus, high-pass again, and keep the clean low-end in the dry bass. Sidechain both the return and the main bass.
Core drums: short punchy kick, short clap, and a very short hi-hat pitched down slightly. Kick gets a small low cut and saturator to bring it forward.
Background groove comes from a steady extra hat plus frequent rimshots. Reuse the same rimshot pitched differently on later hits to get more mileage from one sample.

Layer multiple percussion loops that occupy different frequency roles, high-pass them together so they stay out of the bass, and use Auto Pan / volume shaping to keep them ducked and controlled around the kick.
